does: This paper introduces a three-layer defense framework designed to detect and block both direct and indirect prompt injection attacks in RAG-based chatbots throughout the entire inference pipeline. Layer 1 uses rule-based pattern matching and a fine-tuned semantic anomaly classifier to screen user inputs before processing. Layer 2 implements provenance-based instruction hierarchies during context assembly to ensure retrieved documents cannot override system policies. Layer 3 audits model outputs via policy rule engines and semantic drift detection before responses are delivered to users. The framework addresses a critical gap in existing defenses: while input filters and output monitors typically operate in isolation, this approach covers the full pipeline and specifically handles indirect injection attacks where poisoned knowledge-base documents compromise users across multiple queries.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial attacks against LLMs and prompt injection vulnerabilities, building directly on the problem formalization established by the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications rankings. It responds to the limitations of existing single-stage defenses that fail to address the RAG-specific vulnerability where retrieved documents can introduce malicious instructions. The paper combines multiple defensive techniques including rule-based filtering similar to traditional input sanitization, semantic anomaly detection following advances in out-of-distribution detection for language models, and output monitoring approaches from prior work on LLM safety. The provenance-based instruction hierarchy draws on work in access control and hierarchical policy enforcement adapted for the LLM context.
enables: This framework establishes foundations for more comprehensive RAG security postures that could be deployed across production chatbot systems. The structured logging and continuous audit loop support the development of automated retraining pipelines that adapt defenses to emerging attack patterns, pointing toward self-improving security systems. The layered approach enables future research into optimal detection thresholds and trade-offs between security and utility. The work opens pathways for developing better semantic drift detectors specialized for instruction-following contexts and for designing more sophisticated provenance-tracking systems that maintain security guarantees across complex document chains. It also supports building threat intelligence systems that aggregate injection attempts across deployed systems to identify novel attack patterns.
